risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated randomization with block randomization), which supports low risk of selection bias for this domain.

Quote: "After written informed consent was obtained, the lowest available study number was assigned. A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter." Comment: This describes the randomization sequence and blinding of tablets, but it does not explicitly state a concealment mechanism that prevented the person enrolling participants from knowing the next assignment (e.g. central randomization, SNOSE, or sequentially numbered identical drug containers clearly controlled to prevent foreknowledge). It is therefore unclear whether the enroler could foresee upcoming allocations.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial..." and "Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter." Comment: The report explicitly states the trial was double blind and describes matching placebo tablets identical in appearance, supporting successful blinding of participants and study personnel administering treatment. The main outcomes were largely objective (low birth weight, anaemia/PCV, parasitaemia, placental infection, fetal/neonatal death), so even if blinding were imperfect, these outcomes are not highly susceptible to performance bias.

Quote: "This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial..." and "Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter." Also, outcomes were mainly measured by lab/imaging systems and objective assessors: "Thick and thin blood films were stained and read according to standard, quality-controlled procedures"; "PCV was measured in a microcapillary tube after centrifugation"; "Biopsies were processed and stained following standard procedures"; "Newborns were weighed on a digital scale, accurate to the nearest gram." Comment: Outcome assessors were not separately named, but the trial is explicitly double blind and the reported outcomes relevant here are overwhelmingly objective laboratory/measurement outcomes (parasitaemia, placental malaria, anaemia/PCV, birth weight, deaths). No subjective self-reported or clinician-judgement-dependent outcome appears among the main review outcomes, so lack of assessor blinding is unlikely to have influenced measurement.

Quote: "Figure 1 shows the trial profile..." and "Data were analysed by intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis whereby all randomised women were included regardless of whether or not they had received the intervention and the number of doses." Comment: The article states that a trial profile exists, but the actual CONSORT flow with post-randomization losses is not provided in the supplied text. Outcome tables show varying analysed denominators (for example, low birth weight 59/496 vs 58/494; severe anaemia at delivery 8/495 vs 2/493; postpartum parasitaemia 26/432 vs 13/416) from 515 randomized per arm, indicating missing outcome data, but the paper text supplied does not report per-arm reasons for these losses or clearly link them to specific post-randomization attrition at each analysed time point. Because the necessary reported numbers/reasons for attrition on the analysed outcomes are incomplete, this domain is best judged as unclear rather than high.
